data.title: Unity's Vector continues to expand as it prepares to sunset ironSource: BTIG
data.description:
Unity Software (U) appears poised to continue making gains with Vector, its AI-powered ad platform launched last year, as the company prepares to sunset its ironSource advertising network, according to BTIG.

Shares were up 2.5% during afternoon trading on Monday.

The software company plans to retire the ironSource Ads Network on April 30 and has engaged with financial advisors to assist with the divestiture of its Supersonic game publishing business.

BTIG views the moves as favorable, reiterating its Buy rating and $39 price target on the stock.

"Based on our analysis of the ironSource purchase consideration and '21-22 changes in amortization schedule, we believe there could be up to $221M in amortization savings post-close/sale with de minimis depreciation impact (just $7M of PP&E within the purchase price allocation)," said BTIG analysts Clark Lampen and Joseph Spiezio in a Monday investor note. "Applying those savings to our Vector base and bull case forecasts, we see ~$0.20-0.60 in earnings potential for the Vector operation alone in '27 as non-cash drags moderate."

Unity plans to release its first quarter 2026 financial results on May 7. A consensus estimate expects adjusted earnings per share of $0.24 and revenue of $504.25M.

In March, Unity increased its first-quarter guidance. Unity expects revenue to be between $505M and $508M, compared to guidance of $480M to $490M. Create revenue is expected to be around $115M, while Grow revenue is expected to be around $353M. Analysts had anticipated sales of $488.92M.

data.title: MARA Foundation launch hits stock: Bitcoin's $100K vote war begins
data.description:
MARA Holdings (MARA) announced on Monday the launch of its new MARA Foundation at the Bitcoin 2026 conference in Las Vegas.

The goal is simple: support Bitcoin’s (BTC-USD) long-term growth beyond mining—through research, education, and open-source development.

The foundation will focus on key areas like Bitcoin security (including quantum resistance research), open-source development, global self-custody tools, policy advocacy, and BTC education for developers and regulators.

In short, MARA Holdings (MARA) is trying to strengthen Bitcoin’s (BTC-USD) “base layer” so the network becomes more secure and widely usable over time.

To kick things off, the company also announced a $100K community grant vote, where the Bitcoin (BTC-USD) community will choose one of three organizations:

  • 256 Foundation funds open-source Bitcoin mining hardware/software and developer education.
  • Libreria de Satoshi spreads BTC technical education globally in multiple languages.
  • SateNet builds community-run internet networks in the Global South using BTC support.

This matters because it shows mining giants like MARA (MARA) are moving beyond just earning Bitcoin (BTC-USD)—they are now actively funding the ecosystem that keeps the world's largest cryptocurrency running.

On the treasury side, MARA (MARA) currently holds about 38.689K BTC, making it the 4th largest Bitcoin treasury holder, according to Coingecko. It trails Strategy (MSTR) with ~818.334K BTC, Twenty One Capital (XXI) with ~43.514K, and Metaplanet (MPJPY) with ~40.177K. This puts the company firmly among the top institutional Bitcoin (BTC-USD) holders.

Despite the announcement, the stock fell nearly ~4% to $11.20, showing short-term market pressure.

data.title: Less than 1% of Blue Owl Capital Corporation II investors tender shares to Saba, Cox - report
data.description:
Investors of Blue Owl Capital Corporation II (OBDC II), an affiliate of Blue Owl Capital (OWL), tendered less than 1% of their shares to Saba Capital and Cox Capital Partners, people familiar with the matter told Bloomberg News.

On March 6, activist investor Boaz Weinstein's Saba Capital and Cox Capital Partners announced a tender offer to buy shares of Blue Owl Capital Corporation II for $3.80 per share in cash.

Saba and Cox had offered to purchase up to 8M shares of OBDC II (~6.9% of the outstanding shares). The offer price represented a 34.9% discount to the dividend reinvestment plan issuance value per share disclosed by OBDC II on February 26.

Blue Owl Capital has been battered in recent months. Worries resurfaced when the firm curbed withdrawals from a $1.6B private credit vehicle and sold $1.4B in loans.

Still, OBDC II recommended that its shareholders reject the share purchase offer, which represented a nearly 33% discount to the fund's net asset value. The board believed the offer price stood "well below the potential long-term value of OBDC II shares."

The tender offer that expired at the end of last week wasn't extended, the people who asked not to be named because the information isn't yet public reportedly said.

The scant sales suggest investors prefer to hold onto their shares rather than exit at far less than full value, the Bloomberg report released on Monday, April 27, said.

Saba is considering tender offers for other private credit funds, including Cliffwater's interval fund and Blue Owl Credit Income fund. Blue Owl Credit Income fund had also curbed withdrawals after a surge in redemption requests.

Saba has already added a $40M position in FS KKR Capital (FSK); a $75M stake in Bluerock Private Real Estate Fund (BPRE); and equity stakes in Apollo Global Management (APO), Ares Management (ARES), and Blackstone (BX). 

"The strategy targets entry points at discounts of 30-40% or greater to NAV, a threshold Saba has already achieved across its investments to date, including its $75 million position in BPRE acquired at a 30-40% discount and its tender activities in SREIT," said Saba Capital in a statement.

"Saba believes the question is not whether this space will experience significant stress, but when. Hundreds of billions of dollars of private credit are currently held by retail investors in products that offer limited or no secondary liquidity. Saba intends to be a consistent source of that liquidity—and to have the capital deployed and ready when the need intensifies," said the statement.

data.title: Easterly outlines $3.06-$3.12 2026 core FFO per share outlook as it targets investment-grade rating in 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Easterly Government Properties (DEA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Darrell Crate framed DEA’s positioning around tenant credit and lease duration, saying, "We continue to operate in a market defined by volatility" and adding, "We believe our portfolio is often misclassified alongside traditional office real estate." He highlighted portfolio metrics: "occupancy continues to outpace our REIT peers at 97%" and "weighted average lease terms stood at approximately 9.4 years."

  • Crate emphasized a new capital allocation tool with the company’s first mezzanine investment, stating, "During the quarter, we also completed our first mezzanine investment tied to the development of a new VA outpatient clinic" and that it "is expected to deliver a 12% yield." He also linked external growth to federal priorities: "We also believe that the administration's increased focus on defense spending represents an additional tailwind for the company particularly as it relates to external growth opportunities."

  • Crate reiterated the rating objective, saying, "With a lease portfolio that generates a AA+ revenue stream, we look forward to working with the credit agencies on achieving an investment-grade rating in 2027."

  • CFO Allison Marino reported, "Total revenue increased to $91.5 million" and "EBITDA also grew meaningfully." She added, "On a fully diluted basis, net income per share was $0.03" and "FFO per share increased to $0.76" while "core FFO per share increased to $0.77."

Outlook

  • Marino tied updated full-year guidance to the mezzanine close: "With the successful closing of the mezzanine loan during the quarter, we are raising the low end of our full year guidance by $0.01 from $3.05 to $3.06, resulting in a revised full year range of $3.06 to $3.12."

  • On investment assumptions embedded in guidance, Marino said, "At the midpoint, our guidance assumes that we will have $50 million to $100 million of gross development-related investment during the year and $50 million in wholly-owned acquisitions," while adding, "we continue to take a disciplined and cautious approach... particularly given the ongoing volatility in the interest rate and broader equity market environment."

  • Versus Q4 2025, management moved from "maintaining" the $3.05 to $3.12 range to raising the low end by $0.01, while keeping acquisition and development assumptions unchanged at the midpoint.

Financial Results

  • Marino attributed year-over-year revenue growth to portfolio activity and rent mechanics, saying the increase was "driven primarily by acquisitions completed over the last 12 months, contractual rent growth and continued lease stability across the portfolio." She also disclosed, "Our cash available for distribution was approximately $32.2 million."

  • On leverage and capital markets execution, Marino said, "Our adjusted net debt to annualized quarterly pro forma EBITDA was 7.3x," and explained, "Given the share price volatility... we elected to defer issuing the majority of that equity, and we expect to complete the issuance by the end of the year."

  • On development timing, Marino stated, "Our Fort Myers, Florida lab project is expected to complete and commence its lease in the fourth quarter of 2026," with "the Flagstaff courthouse... scheduled to deliver in the first quarter of 2027" and "the Medford courthouse... anticipated to complete during the second half of 2027."

Q&A

  • Seth Bergey, Citigroup: asked whether mezzanine lending was a one-off and how large it could be; CEO Darrell Crate replied, "I think we could see ourselves allocating about $30 million to this pipeline" and described deployment across "3, 4 projects" over "the next 4, 5, 6 years."

  • Seth Bergey, Citigroup: pressed on catalysts to unlock the $1.5B pipeline; Crate said, "we're also just super judicious about making sure it's accretive" and tied activity to cost of capital improvement and potential rating benefits.

  • John Kim, BMO: asked why not update investment guidance and what spread DEA targets; CFO Allison Marino said, "we target 100 basis point spread to our cost of capital" and added, "we are being conservative" on updating activity guidance until deals are "closer to those deals being cooked."

  • John Kim, BMO: asked how fast DEA could reach $30M of mezz; Crate responded, "Over the next 18 months."

  • Merrill Ross, Compass Point: asked whether VA projects would be JV or wholly owned; Crate answered, "the answer is it could be either," while reiterating the "100 basis point premium to our cost of capital" threshold.

  • Michael Carroll, RBC: asked if DEA has purchase rights on mezz deals; Marino said, "We have both a ROFR and a ROFO on that particular deal." Carroll also questioned deferred equity funding; Crate called it "a pretty marginal comment" and reiterated the leverage goal: "We're going to get something that has a 6 handle on it."

  • Joseph Dickstein, Jefferies: asked what metrics matter for investment grade; Crate emphasized leverage and stated, "if we get into the 6s" the company is better positioned, adding, "we think 2027 is hopefully our year." Dickstein also asked what share price enables more activity; Crate said, "every little bit of share price will obviously make it easier" while stressing caution on setting expectations.

  • Michael Lewis, Truist: asked whether mezz becomes the preferred way to do development; Crate replied, "It depends on the project" and contrasted internal development of FDA labs with partnering on VA projects. Lewis also pressed on conservatism in guidance; Marino cited rate volatility, saying, "interest rates are like really wacky right now," and flagged project timing sensitivity for Fort Myers.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative to neutral analyst tone centered on capital allocation pacing, conservatism in guidance, and execution path to growth, with repeated probing on why acquisition guidance was not raised and how interest-rate volatility could constrain results.

  • Slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks, turning more cautious in Q&A on near-term funding markets; Marino explicitly pointed to uncertainty: "interest rates are like really wacky right now," while Crate remained forward-leaning on growth tools and pipeline.

  • Compared with Q4 2025, management shifted from emphasizing DOGE as "in the rearview mirror" to emphasizing market volatility and alternative structures (mezzanine) to generate accretive growth amid cost-of-capital constraints.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance language became incrementally more constructive: Q4 2025 kept $3.05 to $3.12, while Q1 2026 raised the low end to $3.06 and repeated a "disciplined and cautious" posture on the remainder of the year.

  • Strategy discussion broadened from traditional acquisitions/development to new financing structures: Q1 introduced mezzanine as "adjacent opportunities" and repeatedly framed it as a way to stay engaged with future-ownable assets while spreads are attractive.

  • Analyst focus widened from acquisition pipeline visibility (Q4) to mechanics of mezz deployment, ROFR/ROFO protections, equity deferral, and whether 2027 growth could be flat; management repeatedly declined to quantify 2027 but argued flat growth "would make no sense."

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted capital markets and rates as key constraints; Marino said, "interest rates are like really wacky right now" and noted equity issuance timing was deferred due to "share price volatility."

  • Leasing upside is not guaranteed and is time-consuming with federal tenants; Crate said vacant space pursuits "can be 6 to 9 months kind of things" and referenced unleased space in Atlanta: "this FDA lab in Atlanta that we just opened have tens of thousands of square feet that are not leased."

  • Execution risk remains on development delivery timing; Marino pointed to remaining "development risk" ahead of the Fort Myers Q4 2026 commencement.

Final Takeaway

Management described a Q1 setup built on high occupancy, long lease duration, and government-credit tenancy, while leaning more visibly on mezzanine financing as an accretive growth tool amid equity and rate volatility. The company raised the low end of 2026 core FFO per share guidance to $3.06, kept midpoint investment assumptions intact, and framed deleveraging toward a “6 handle” as central to its stated goal of pursuing an investment-grade rating in 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nutrien raised to Buy at Barclays on continued nitrogen strength from Iran war
data.description:
Nutrien (NTR) up 0.9% in Monday's trading as Barclays upgraded the world's largest producer of potash and second-largest producer of nitrogen fertilizer to Overweight from Equal Weight with an $85 price target, bumped up from $80, reflecting prolonged uncertainty around the Iran war, combined with an expected longer-lasting disruption to natural gas supply and its implications for ammonia production.

Barclays analyst Benjamin Theurer cited stronger ammonia pricing, with some support from potash—which has moved recently from levels of ~$350/metric ton to ~$400/ton—as a leading indicator, which results in adjusted EBITDA of more than $7.3B in FY 2026, up 21% Y/Y, compared to his previous estimate of $6.6B.

The analyst also reiterated his Overweight rating for CF Industries (CF) while hiking his stock price target to $145 from $130, now modeling for adjusted EBITDA of more than $5B in FY 2026 vs. his former estimate of $3.3B.

Looking to FY 2027, Theurer said he expects higher ammonia/urea pricing to persist at least to H1 2027, resulting in revised adjusted EBITDA for CF (CF) to $3.6B from $2.6B previously.

data.title: Noble outlines 2026 adjusted EBITDA of $940M-$1.02B as backlog stands at $7.5B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Noble Corporation (NE) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "During the first quarter, we earned adjusted EBITDA of $277 million and generated free cash flow of $169 million," said "President, CEO & Director Robert Eifler." He added, "We again distributed our $0.50 quarterly dividend and yesterday, our Board declared a $0.50 per share dividend for the second quarter, maintaining our consistent and highly differentiated return of cash strategy."

  • "President, CEO & Director Robert Eifler" described limited Middle East disruption tied to the Iran conflict, stating, "we are fortunate to have experienced limited operational disruption confined to just 1 jackup in the Middle East, the Mick O'Brien, which we sold in January but have continued to operate under a bareboat agreement." He added, "All of our crew and related personnel were safely evacuated from the rig during the early days of the conflict."

  • "President, CEO & Director Robert Eifler" detailed roughly "$565 million" of new awards and extensions, including: "the Noble Courage received an extension with Petrobras of slightly more than 3 years"; "the Noble Deliverer has been awarded a 5-well contract from Woodside in Australia, which will support that rig's reactivation"; and "the Noble Developer has been awarded a 1-well contract with ExxonMobil at $375,000 per day." He also cited incremental work for "the Noble BlackRhino," "the Noble Venturer" ("at a day rate of $430,000"), and "the Noble Viking" in Malaysia.

  • "President, CEO & Director Robert Eifler" said market indicators were strengthening, including that "Total UDW contracted utilization is currently 105 rigs or 95% of marketed supply" and open demand "has now eclipsed 110 rig years." He concluded, "Therefore, we believe it is likely that we will begin to see floater rates move higher as we move through the rest of this year."

  • "Executive VP & CFO Richard Barker" provided the quarter’s financial snapshot: "Contract Drilling Services revenue for the first quarter totaled $742 million. Adjusted EBITDA was $277 million and adjusted EBITDA margin was 35%. Q1 cash flow from operations was $273 million, capital expenditures were $104 million and free cash flow was $169 million."

Outlook

  • "Executive VP & CFO Richard Barker" said guidance was unchanged: "we are maintaining full year 2026 guidance for total revenue between $2.8 billion and $3 billion which includes approximately $150 million in reimbursable and other revenue and adjusted EBITDA between $940 million to $1.02 billion."

  • "Executive VP & CFO Richard Barker" flagged a CapEx change and specific offsets: "Capital expenditures guidance for this year has increased by $25 million and this is due to the contract awards supporting the reactivation of the Noble Deliverer." He added that stronger Q1 EBITDA was offset by "a recent notice of early contract termination on the Mick O'Brien," "the lower near-term day rate revision resulting from the Courage's blend and extend" and "slightly later estimated contract commencement dates for the Gerry de Souza and Endeavor driven by customer schedules."

  • Compared with the prior quarter, the guidance ranges for 2026 revenue and adjusted EBITDA were reiterated at the same levels in Q1 2026 and Q4 2025.

Financial Results

  • "Executive VP & CFO Richard Barker" said Noble received asset-sale proceeds and executed financing actions: "we received $210 million in cash proceeds from the jackup sale to Borr Drilling" and "redeemed $55 million principal amount of the 8.5% senior secured notes at 103 as an opportunistic and efficient use of capital."

  • "Executive VP & CFO Richard Barker" described the BOP lease buyouts and timing: "we completed the lease buyout on the first 2 of the 4 Black ships BOP systems to $36.5 million" and "The buyout of the remaining 2 BOP systems is expected to occur during Q2 and Q4 this year for approximately $18 million each," adding "In total, the lease buyout for all 4 systems is expected to cost $73 million." (He also noted the cash outflows are "not part of capital expenditures" but in "financing activities.")

  • "Executive VP & CFO Richard Barker" quantified the Mick O’Brien impact from early release: "this will result in an estimated negative impact of approximately $15 million due to our remaining bareboat obligations through early December as well as stacking costs for the rig."

Q&A

  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: Asked how energy security concerns could affect deepwater demand and whether majors could revive projects; "President, CEO & Director Eifler" answered, "we don't have necessarily really tangible evidence today of positive changes that have hit us" but added, "we don't really see any way that this doesn't turn out positively for our business."

  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: Asked about OpEx impact from BOP lease buy-ins; "Executive VP & CFO Barker" said, "on an annualized basis, it will have a benefit to EBITDA of about $25 million" and "in 2026, probably about half of that will be realized."

  • Scott Gruber, Citigroup: Asked if customer conversations indicate incremental exploration or infill; "President, CEO & Director Eifler" replied, "there is an increase in narrative around -- and discussion around exploration work" but "I'm not sure that there's a direct link so far."

  • Scott Gruber, Citigroup: Asked if Deliverer reactivation spend is fully captured; "Executive VP & CFO Barker" responded, "the $25 million is the total required for the Woodside contract" and added potential "incremental rig upgrades" would be additional.

  • Edward Kim, Barclays: Asked about 2027 floater day rates and Petrobras extension volumes; "President, CEO & Director Eifler" said, "we're pretty optimistic about a really tight market" and on Petrobras, "this is in line with our expectations."

  • Keith Beckmann, Pickering: Asked which regions could see tender conversion and then asked about BlackRhino/Globetrotter I/Apex outlook; "President, CEO & Director Eifler" highlighted "Asia" and "West Africa" and said BlackRhino "could very easily stay in the U.S." while Globetrotter I remains "focused on intervention work" and Apex options are being evaluated.

  • Fredrik Stene, Clarksons: Asked whether Faye Kozack was part of Petrobras blend-and-extend and about debt repurchases; "President, CEO & Director Eifler" said, "the Faye Kozack is not a part of the blend and extend" and "Executive VP & CFO Barker" said the buyback was enabled by "a specific clause" and added, "at the right time, we'll definitely refinance the capital structure and collapse that back... into one silo."

  • Doug Becker, Capital One: Asked about drillship upgrades and what’s needed to hit the high end of guidance; "President, CEO & Director Eifler" said, "All of the rigs will have MPD here in the not-too-distant future" and "by and large, we're pretty happy with where everything sits right now," while "Executive VP & CFO Barker" pointed to "great uptime" and said BlackRhino opportunities could help reach "the high end of the range."

  • Aaron Rosenthal, JPMorgan: Asked for more detail on Mick O’Brien; "Executive VP & CFO Barker" said the "$15 million" reflects "about 6 months of the bareboat charter plus stacking costs" and added, "We don't see any other impact to our financials."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive with probing around oil-price-driven demand, Petrobras, supply chain/logistics, and idle rig visibility, including rate-setting questions such as whether "by some time in 2027, we could be back up into the mid- to high 400s" (Edward Kim, Barclays).

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and opportunity-focused on tightening utilization and backlog, while remaining cautious on causality from geopolitical events, including "we don't have necessarily really tangible evidence today" and "I'm not sure that there's a direct link so far" ("President, CEO & Director Eifler").

  • Versus last quarter, prepared-remarks confidence on market tightening was stronger in Q1 2026, including "we feel better about 2027 today versus last quarter" ("President, CEO & Director Eifler"), while Q4 2025 included more conditional framing around crude and timing risk, such as "Not a day, I don't wake up concerned about things getting pushed to the right" ("President, CEO & Director Eifler").

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 maintained the same 2026 guidance ranges that were introduced in Q4 2025, while adding a specific increase in 2026 CapEx tied to Deliverer reactivation.

  • Q1 2026 reframed the macro backdrop around "extreme volatility" and the "Iran conflict" ("President, CEO & Director Eifler"), whereas Q4 2025 emphasized "macro uncertainties" with Brent between "$60 and $70 per barrel" and discussed the gap between present utilization ("82%") and contracted utilization ("95%") as a driver of "soft day rates" ("President, CEO & Director Eifler").

  • On Brazil/Petrobras, Q4 2025 described ongoing "constructive dialogue" to extend the "Faye Kozack" and "Courage"; Q1 2026 announced the Courage extension details and stated "the Faye Kozack is not a part of the blend and extend" ("President, CEO & Director Eifler").

Risks and Concerns

  • "Executive VP & CFO Richard Barker" identified a discrete downside item: "notice of early release" on the Mick O’Brien, with an estimated (-$15 million) impact tied to bareboat and stacking costs.

  • "President, CEO & Director Robert Eifler" described operational and supply-chain pressure tied to the Strait of Hormuz impact, stating, "logistics are strained" and "we're really focused on timing right now."

  • "President, CEO & Director Robert Eifler" emphasized that customer discipline remains a gating factor, saying, "we certainly wouldn't expect our customers to waver from their commitment to discipline and our optimism does not require them to abandon any discipline."

Final Takeaway

Noble management characterized Q1 2026 as a "solid start" with $277 million of adjusted EBITDA and $169 million of free cash flow, reinforced by a maintained $0.50 quarterly dividend and $7.5 billion backlog. The company reiterated its 2026 revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance ranges, raised 2026 capital expenditure guidance by $25 million for the Noble Deliverer reactivation, and outlined both tightening deepwater utilization and expanding tender visibility, while also flagging a specific (-$15 million) impact from an early termination on the Mick O’Brien and continued execution risk from strained logistics and timing.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bank of Marin expects solid 2026 loan growth following $16.3M nonaccrual loan sales
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bank of Marin Bancorp (BMRC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Timothy Myers said the quarter reflected “continued improvement in year-over-year profitability metrics, loan production, net interest margin expansion and improved credit quality,” and added that “compared to the first quarter of 2025 net income and earnings per share grew by 75% and 77%, respectively.”
  • Myers highlighted loan production and mix, saying, “we originated $81 million in new loans $61 million of which was funded,” and described demand as “our strongest first quarter in a number of years,” with production “slight skewing towards C&I.”
  • On credit actions, Myers said, “we sold our longest tenure classified and nonaccrual loans totaling $16.3 million,” adding “the charge-offs equaling the specific amounts reserved,” and noted nonaccrual loans “declined from 1.27% of assets to 0.41%.”
  • CFO Dave Bonaccorso reported, “our net income was $8.5 million or $0.53 per share,” and said net interest income “increased from the prior quarter to $30.3 million,” with “a 6 basis point increase in our net interest margin.”
  • Bonaccorso emphasized balance-sheet tactics on deposits and noninterest income, stating, “we moved a portion of these funds off balance sheet to take advantage of a relatively high one-way sell rate,” and added, “this opportunity has persisted into Q2.”

Outlook

  • Myers said, “we continue to see stable economic conditions in our markets,” and framed 2026 priorities around growth, stating, “we continue to expect to generate solid loan growth in 2026,” alongside deposit growth “through the addition of new relationships and expansion of existing client relationships.”
  • On net interest margin trajectory, Bonaccorso told analysts, “on a full year basis, mid-3s is probably still appropriate,” and characterized the cadence as “a handful of basis points a quarter,” citing “decent tailwinds with regard to loan repricing.”
  • On expenses, Bonaccorso said, “we otherwise expect noninterest expense to continue near current levels as we continue to invest in people and technology,” while Myers added that management would be “opportunistic” on hires that “can help us drive the growth.”

Financial Results

  • Bonaccorso said, “our net income was $8.5 million or $0.53 per share,” and reported net interest income of “$30.3 million,” citing drivers including “higher investment security yields and reduce deposit costs” and “positive churn in the loan portfolio.”
  • On margin normalization, Bonaccorso said, “adjusting for the fourth quarter recovery of interest and fees on a paid off nonaccrual loan relationship our sequential quarter net interest margin growth would have been even more impressive at 14 basis points.”
  • Bonaccorso said fee income had notable one-offs, stating, “we did receive a special dividend on FHLB stock as well as BOLI death benefit,” which “positively impacted our total noninterest income.”
  • On credit costs and reserves, Bonaccorso stated, “we did not require a provision for credit losses in the first quarter,” and said “our allowance for credit losses remained strong at 1.08% of total loans.”
  • On capital return, Bonaccorso said, “our Board of Directors declared a cash dividend of $0.25 per share,” calling it “the 84th consecutive quarterly dividend.”

Q&A

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: “How much was the interest reversal…?” Dave Bonaccorso: “in Q4, it was, I believe, $667,000,” and Timothy Myers clarified, “part of the decline was impacted by that $670,000 interest accrual reversal in Q4.”
  • Clark, Piper Sandler: deposit cost path and marginal costs; Bonaccorso said the bank would make “targeted adjustments away from Fed cuts,” and pointed to “a 24 basis point decline sequential quarter” from “time deposit repricing,” while Myers cited pressure from “large existing clients” and said they were “managing with one-way sells.”
  • Clark, Piper Sandler: buyback timing; Myers said, “it’s a conversation we’re going to start having,” but added, “I don’t want to overpromise,” while noting the credit sale “remove[d] a big hurdle.”
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: terminal margin level; Bonaccorso said, “mid-3s is probably still appropriate,” and described quarterly progress as “a few basis points.”
  • Rulis, D.A. Davidson: remaining nonaccrual and CRE market; Myers said the remaining issue loan was “a dispute over terms of an extension,” adding, “we don’t have any loss expectations,” and on San Francisco said, “the trends remain very positive… driven by AI-related investments.”
  • Wood Lay, KBW: expense jump and operating leverage; Myers cited “annual resets,” “higher level of our annual charitable giving,” and an “outlier… FDIC insurance expense,” while Bonaccorso responded on leverage, “Yes, I agree with that.”
  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: loan repricing details and capital targets; Bonaccorso said new loan yields were “[5.91%]… compar[ed] to [5.51%] for paid off loans,” and added, “we have about 17% of the portfolio repricing in the next year and 34% over the next 3 years,” while Myers said, “we really haven’t established a level where we need to be” for CET1/leverage.
  • David Feaster, Raymond James: drivers of origination growth and fee/M&A focus; Myers said production was mostly from “those hires,” credited Sacramento leadership changes, and said incentives reduced “caps,” while on fee initiatives he referenced “treasury management, fee income, wealth management trust income,” and on M&A said, “we remain open to” it as valuation improves.
  • Tim Coffey, Brean: spread compression and portfolio mix; Myers said “there’s been pretty incredible compression in pricing,” cited market bids at “1.5 to 1.75,” and reiterated preference for more C&I: “I would love to do a higher proportion of C&I.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, pressing on margin durability, expense run-rate, capital deployment, and buybacks, including Rulis asking for a margin level “indicative of the balance sheet today” and Clark probing why buybacks have paused.
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, with Myers using cautionary phrasing on capital actions (“I don’t want to overpromise”) and refusing explicit capital targets (“we really haven’t established a level”).
  • Versus last quarter’s emphasis on executing the restructuring, the current call’s confidence focused more on credit cleanup and core earnings momentum, while still remaining guarded on capital actions and explicit guidance.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 centered on credit resolution and normalized earnings, with Myers saying, “we sold… nonaccrual loans totaling $16.3 million,” and Bonaccorso reporting “no… provision for credit losses,” while Q4 focused on the balance-sheet repositioning that “did result in a net loss” (Myers) and included “a net loss of $39.5 million or $2.49 per share” tied to “the $69 million loss” on the repositioning (Bonaccorso).
  • Credit metrics tightened quarter-over-quarter, with Myers reporting nonaccrual loans “declined… to 0.41%,” compared with Q4’s “1.3% of total loans” (Myers, prior call), and the ACL ratio moving to “1.08% of total loans” (Bonaccorso, current call) from “1.42%” (Bonaccorso, prior call).
  • Analyst focus shifted from restructuring mechanics and deposit repricing lag (Q4) toward post-cleanup capital flexibility, buybacks, and sustainable margin levels (Q1).

Risks and Concerns

  • Myers flagged competitive loan pricing pressure, saying, “there’s been pretty incredible compression in pricing,” and noted the bank “regularly see[s] people bidding at the 1.5 to 1.75 level,” while stating the bank would “not race to the bottom.”
  • Myers described an industry-specific weak spot: “Wine is a big weak spot,” while adding, “we’re managing that well and our exposure is not all that big there anymore.”
  • On the remaining nonaccrual, Myers said it is tied to “a legal process,” though he emphasized, “we don’t have any loss expectations on that credit.”

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a pivot from last quarter’s restructuring-driven noise toward core performance, highlighting $8.5 million of net income ($0.53 per share), improved net interest income, and a material reduction in nonaccrual assets after selling $16.3 million of long-tenured problem credits. Executives repeatedly pointed to loan repricing tailwinds and disciplined underwriting amid aggressive market pricing, while remaining cautious on buyback timing and explicit capital targets as they rebuild bank-level capital ratios and pursue “solid loan growth in 2026.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.description:
• Bank of Marin Bancorp press release (BMRC): Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.53 misses by $0.02.
  • Net interest income of $30.3M.
  • Total cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash were $236.6M.
